questions.scenario:
A support note says the app wrote this key:

```text
receipts/2026/05/02/order_ord_8x7k2n/devpolaris-orders-receipt.pdf
```

The download code asks for:

```text
receipt/2026/05/02/order_ord_8x7k2n/devpolaris-orders-receipt.pdf
```

What is the most likely problem?

options.text: List the plural prefix, but keep the reader's singular key-building rule unchanged if the object appears.
The download code is asking for a different key because `receipts/` and `receipt/` are different strings.
The object was written with the wrong content type, which changes the prefix S3 uses for retrieval.
The reader may lack `s3:ListBucket`, causing S3 to normalize the singular prefix during authorization.

questions.explanation:
S3 treats nearby key names as different object names. The two strings identify different objects, even though they look almost the same. The first fix is to make the writer and reader use the same key-building rule.

Learn more in [Amazon S3 object keys](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-keys.html).

questions.scenario:
A finance reader has `s3:GetObject` on `exports/daily/*`, but reading one export still fails:

```text
errorName=AccessDenied
bucket=devpolaris-orders-prod-objects
key=exports/daily/2026/05/02/orders.csv
encryption=aws:kms
kmsKey=arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abcd-1234-example
```

What is the best next check?

options.text: Rotate the KMS key because an S3 `AccessDenied` on an encrypted object indicates that the current key material is unusable.
Broaden the reader's `s3:GetObject` resource to the whole bucket before checking its KMS permissions.
Check whether the caller is allowed to use the KMS key for decrypting this object.
Copy the object with SSE-S3 encryption so the existing reader permission can work without KMS review.

questions.explanation:
With SSE-KMS, S3 permission may not be enough. The caller also needs permission through the KMS key policy or grants. Broadening S3 prefixes does not fix missing KMS decrypt permission and may expose data finance should not read.

Learn more in [AWS KMS key policies](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html).

questions.scenario:
Finance reports this missing file:

```text
requested=s3://devpolaris-orders-prod-objects/exports/daily/2026/05/02/devpolaris-orders-export-2026-05-02.csv
head-object=404 Not Found
callerListBucket=Allowed for prefix exports/* and export/*
appLogKey=export/daily/2026/05/02/devpolaris-orders-export-2026-05-02.csv
```

What is the best diagnosis?

options.text: The caller has list permission for the relevant prefix, so the 404 supports an exact-key mismatch rather than a hidden authorization denial.
List only the requested `exports/daily/` prefix and treat an empty result as proof the worker never wrote an object.
The object may exist under the wrong prefix, so compare and list the exact written prefix.
The worker may have written the correct key in a different Region, so compare bucket Region before listing the logged prefix.

questions.explanation:
The requested key and application key differ by one letter in the prefix. Because the caller has `s3:ListBucket` for both relevant prefixes, the 404 supports absence at the requested exact key rather than the permission-dependent 403 behavior. Listing the logged `export/daily/` prefix can confirm whether the object was misplaced.

Learn more in [HeadObject error behavior](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/API_HeadObject.html).

questions.scenario: Finance reports that one export is missing. The writer logged only a nearby prefix, and the support role has `s3:GetObject` for that prefix but does not have `s3:ListBucket`. The engineer must determine whether the application wrote a different key or whether authorization blocks the read without broadening production access. Which diagnostic sequence is best?

options.text: Confirm the exact bucket, Region, key, and caller; use `head-object` on the candidate key; interpret 403 or 404 with the caller's `s3:ListBucket` permission; then use a narrowly scoped prefix listing through an approved identity if the key remains uncertain.
Grant the support role temporary `s3:*`, list the entire bucket, and remove the permission after the object is found.
Treat a 403 from `head-object` as proof that the object exists, then change only the object's ACL.
Treat a 404 from `head-object` as proof that the object was never written, regardless of the caller's bucket permissions.

questions.explanation:
The sequence preserves least privilege while distinguishing key mismatch from authorization failure. `HeadObject` can return 404 for a missing key when the caller can list the bucket and 403 when the caller cannot, so neither status should be interpreted without permission context. Broad `s3:*`, ACL changes, or full-bucket downloads are not justified by the evidence.

Learn more in [HeadObject error behavior](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/API_HeadObject.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris stores customer receipts in a versioned S3 bucket. Support must recover a receipt that was overwritten yesterday, while temporary export chunks under `tmp/exports/` should expire after seven days and current receipts must remain available. Which design best separates recovery from cleanup?

options.text: Disable versioning before adding lifecycle rules so expiration cannot create delete markers.
Recover the required receipt by version ID, and use prefix-scoped lifecycle rules that expire temporary chunks without applying the same rule to receipt versions.
Apply one seven-day expiration rule to the whole bucket, then restore receipts from CloudWatch Logs when needed.
Copy every overwritten receipt into a new bucket manually and leave temporary chunks without expiration.

questions.explanation:
Versioning preserves prior object versions that support can retrieve explicitly. Lifecycle rules should reflect data purpose, so disposable temporary prefixes can expire without applying the same retention to customer receipts. Disabling versioning removes recovery protection, a bucket-wide rule ignores distinct obligations, and logs are not receipt backups.

Learn more in [S3 Versioning](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/Versioning.html) and [S3 lifecycle management](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-lifecycle-mgmt.html).

questions.scenario:
After a release, upload fails with:

```text
errorName=AccessDenied
action=s3:PutObject
caller=arn:aws:sts::123456789012:assumed-role/devpolaris-orders-api-prod-task-role/ecs-task-72af
resource=arn:aws:s3:::devpolaris-orders-prod-objects/export/daily/2026/05/02/orders.csv
allowedResource=arn:aws:s3:::devpolaris-orders-prod-objects/exports/daily/*
```

What should the team check first?

options.text: Broaden the task role to both singular and plural prefixes before confirming whether the release changed the key contract.
Change the IAM policy to permit both singular and plural prefixes, then fix the application later.
Treat the singular prefix as a new export class even though the release record and allowed resource name only the plural prefix.
Whether the release changed the prefix from `exports/daily/` to `export/daily/`.

questions.explanation:
The denied request tells a clear story: caller, action, and resource. The resource uses a different prefix than the allowed policy. The safe first move is to fix or confirm the prefix, not to widen permissions.

Learn more in [Amazon S3 access management](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/access-management.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris lets customers download private receipt PDFs without giving browsers AWS credentials. A proposed API returns a reusable public S3 URL, but security requires access to expire after five minutes and remain scoped to one object and HTTP operation. Which solution best meets the requirement?

options.text: Generate a five-minute presigned `GetObject` URL for the authorized receipt after the application checks ownership.
Make the receipt prefix public and hide object keys behind unguessable UUIDs.
Send the ECS task role access keys to the browser so the AWS SDK can call `GetObject` directly.
Create one presigned URL with a one-year expiry and reuse it for all receipts under the customer prefix.

questions.explanation:
A presigned URL delegates one bounded S3 operation using the signing principal's permissions, so the application should authorize ownership first and issue a short-lived URL for the exact object. Public prefixes and browser credentials expand trust unnecessarily, while a long-lived reusable URL does not meet the expiry or object-scope requirement.

Learn more in [S3 presigned URLs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/using-presigned-url.html).
